O-01.name: Supply chain concentration & disruption

supplier_recovery_runway_weeks.source: erp_supply_chain.csv — TSMC critical-path supplier (18-week lead time)
supplier_recovery_runway_weeks.description: Inventory cover weeks for the critical-path single-source supplier (TSMC, 18-week lead time). Amber threshold 8w = TSMC target_inventory_weeks from ERP. Breach threshold 4w = minimum safe operating level (~22% of TSMC lead time). Current 5.2w = amber: 2.8 weeks below target, 12.8 weeks below TSMC lead time — a TSMC disruption today results in production stop in 5.2 weeks with no substitution path.
supplier_recovery_runway_weeks.threshold_rationale: TSMC 18-week lead time benchmark: amber at target inventory (8w), breach at 22% of lead time (4w)

O-01.agent_findings:
OPERATIONAL RISK ASSESSMENT — 2026-05-28

This assessment covers three confirmed escalation conditions across O-01, O-02, and O-04. The aggregate picture is one of compounding operational fragility: supply chain concentration is structurally above breach thresholds with a distressed single-source supplier at minimum inventory, cyber defences are materially weakened by unpatched systems and zero supplier-side visibility, and talent losses in R&D and Engineering are beginning to erode the control environment itself. The stress model quantifies revenue_at_risk_usd_b=9.1 and var_95_baseline_usd_m=2,634 from supply disruption alone.

O-01 SUPPLY CHAIN — BREACH (ESCALATION TRIGGERED)
Single-source concentration stands at 62.6% of $12,610M total spend, against a breach threshold of 50%. Three suppliers — TSMC [$4,200M, Semiconductors-CPU], Foxconn [$2,100M, Manufacturing-Assembly], and Quanta [$1,600M, ODM-Laptops] — together represent $7,900M of spend with no alternative source. The most acute risk is Quanta [SUP005]: financial_health of 61 (below the 65 escalation threshold), inventory cover of 3.9 weeks (below the 4-week breach threshold), OTD of 88.7% (lowest of the three), and a rejection rate of 1.2% (highest of the three). The simultaneous occurrence of single-source dependency, financial distress, and sub-threshold inventory for Quanta meets the compound escalation trigger and should be treated as an immediate board-level concern.

TSMC carries an 18-week lead time and Taiwan represents 53.5% of total spend. Any Taiwan corridor disruption — geopolitical, logistical, or natural — cannot be absorbed within a 3.9-week inventory buffer. The stress model recovery period of 5 months confirms that no rapid substitution is possible; the organisation would face a production gap of multiple quarters. Revenue at risk is quantified at $9.1B by the supply chain stress model.

The geographic concentration profile adds systematic amplification: Taiwan (53.5%) and China (21.8%) together represent 75.3% of spend. TSMC (Taiwan), Quanta (Taiwan), and Foxconn (China) are all single-source, meaning a regional event could simultaneously impair all three critical supply lines. Samsung, LG Chem, Pegatron, Flex Ltd, and Yangtze Memory are multi-sourced, but Yangtze Memory [SUP008] carries a financial_health of 55 — the weakest score in the entire supplier portfolio — and warrants enhanced monitoring and contingency sourcing despite its multi-source status.

The stress model also identifies saving_dual_src_usd_m=382 and saving_inv_buff_usd_m=305 as the two highest-return risk mitigations available. Initiating dual-source qualification for Quanta and building inventory toward the 6-week amber threshold are the priority actions.

O-02 CYBER AND IT — BREACH (ESCALATION TRIGGERED)
Four KRIs are in breach: MTTD at 11 days (breach threshold 10 days), patch compliance at 71% (breach threshold 80%), OMS RTO at 6 hours (breach threshold 6 hours — at limit), and supplier cyber resilience assessment at 0% with 4 active security incidents MTD. The 0% supplier assessment combined with confirmed active incidents this period meets the escalation trigger.

MTTD of 11 days represents an average dwell time that allows a sophisticated threat actor to establish persistence, move laterally, and exfiltrate data or pre-position for a destructive event before detection. With patch compliance at 71% — leaving 29% of systems without current patches — and 8 critical vulnerabilities open beyond 30 days, the attack surface is materially open. Privileged access has gone unreviewed for 45 days, creating an additional vector for account compromise or insider exploitation.

The 0% supplier cyber resilience assessment is arguably the most strategically dangerous finding in O-02. The organisation has no visibility into the cyber posture of any supplier, including TSMC, Foxconn, and Quanta — three single-source suppliers carrying $7,900M of spend. A cyber incident originating in the supply chain and propagating into manufacturing execution or order management systems would simultaneously trigger O-01 and O-02 consequences, including potential supply disruption and OMS downtime. With OMS RTO at exactly the breach threshold of 6 hours, there is no recovery headroom.

MFA coverage at 87% and third-party vendors assessed at 62% are both amber. The combination of 13% MFA gap, 45-day unreviewed privileged access, and 0% supplier assessment creates a layered authentication and perimeter weakness that is consistent with — and likely contributing to — the 4 security incidents recorded this month.

O-03 PRODUCT QUALITY — AMBER (NO ESCALATION)
There are no safety incidents YTD and no regulatory product notices YTD. The zero-tolerance safety threshold is not breached and this is the one area of relative comfort in this assessment. Field failure rate at 0.019% is well below both the amber (0.05%) and breach (0.10%) thresholds, and first-pass yield at 97.2% is healthy.

However, three amber conditions warrant forward-looking attention. First, the recall readiness score of 72 is only 2 points above the breach threshold of 70, and the last recall simulation ran 8 months ago. An organisation with 1,842 warranty claims MTD and a supplier rejection rate of 0.68% (amber) should be in a state of active recall readiness, not declining away from it. Second, Quanta [SUP005]'s rejection rate of 1.2% is the primary driver of supply-side quality pressure. If Quanta's financial distress translates into further quality deterioration, the supplier rejection rate could approach the breach threshold of 1.0% on an aggregate basis. Third, the direct connection between Quanta's quality and O-01's financial distress risk means quality risk and supply continuity risk are co-located in the same supplier — a dual-source qualification would address both simultaneously.

The single near-miss event YTD should be formally reviewed in the context of the declining recall readiness score to ensure there is no developing quality systemic issue below the threshold of regulatory notice.

O-04 TALENT — BREACH (ESCALATION TRIGGERED)
Three flight-risk employees are flagged in R&D — a single critical function — meeting the escalation trigger. This is not an abstract risk: R&D staff carry product design knowledge, safety specification expertise, and programme delivery capability that cannot be transferred in weeks. Their departure would directly impair the product roadmap and, in the context of a 72 recall readiness score, the organisation's ability to respond to a quality event.

The structural driver is clear: Engineering compensation is 8.2% below market, Engineering attrition is 14.2% annualised (amber), and the counter-offer acceptance rate is only 42% — meaning even when the organisation attempts to retain staff, more than half leave anyway. This signals that compensation is not the only retention lever; engagement, career progression, and role quality are also factors, consistent with an employee engagement score of 67%.

Seven critical roles have been open beyond 60 days (breach threshold: 6), with an average fill time of 68 days in Engineering. The organisation is not filling roles fast enough to offset attrition. SVP succession coverage at 72% (amber, deteriorating) means leadership continuity is also exposed.

The talent situation in IT-Operations (11.8% attrition, amber) directly impairs the organisation's ability to close the O-02 cyber control gaps. Remediating 8 critical vulnerabilities, closing a 29-percentage-point patch compliance gap, and running a privileged access review all require IT-Operations capacity that is being eroded by attrition. This is the clearest example in this assessment of a talent risk becoming a cyber control risk.

O-02.name: Cyber attack & IT resilience

cyber_supply_response_gap_days.description: Days by which combined MTTD+MTTR cyber response time exceeds available inventory cover. Positive value = production halt guaranteed if cyber incident disrupts supplier comms. Amber: 0 (response just meets inventory); Breach: >0 (response exceeds inventory). Target: ≤ -2 days (2-day safety margin).

O-03.override_justification: Expert override applied 2026-06-07 (EM-08 panel finding). Arithmetic rating: low (l=2, i=5). Override to: high (l=3, i=5). Justification: Likelihood score of 2 is inconsistent with active breach conditions — warranty_claims_mtd at 1,842 (up from 1,612), recall_readiness at 72 (amber, 2 points from breach), recall simulation last run 8 months ago. Upstream supply quality (Quanta rejection rate 1.2%, Foxconn 0.8%) is a confirmed leading driver of downstream field defects. Per O-04 override precedent: forward-looking indicators and breach trajectory take precedence over lagging control effectiveness score. CRO sign-off required next review cycle.

O-04.name: Talent retention & key-person risk
O-04.override_justification: Expert: High. Arithmetic: Low (+2 bands). Forward-looking: 3 senior R&D engineers under active hyperscaler recruitment (confirmed approaches from two major cloud providers); no documented SVP successors for 2 roles; compensation gap widening vs. hyperscaler benchmarks. Override reflects leading indicators not captured in lagging control effectiveness score. CRO sign-off: 2026-06-07 (reissued EM-06 panel correction — prior text erroneously referenced 'senior traders'; corrected to R&D engineering population).

appetite_thresholds.domain_statement: Low appetite for supply chain disruption and product quality failures. Zero tolerance for major safety incidents. Low tolerance for cyber incidents: MTTD must stay below 5 days; patch compliance must exceed 95%.

## Financial Risk Domain Assessment — 2026-05-28

### F-01: FX & COMMODITY EXPOSURE

**Scope clarification:** Per CF-04 (2026-06-07), F-01 KRI scope is FX-only. Commodity figures are reported separately as supplementary context for EBITDA covenant stress analysis.

**FX Portfolio Summary (KRI scope):**
Total unhedged FX exposure stands at USD 4,080M against an amber threshold of USD 4,000M, placing this KRI in amber status. The aggregate FX hedge ratio is 46.3%, above the breach threshold of 45% but below the 60% amber trigger. FX unrealised P&L is USD -26.6M across four currency pairs.

**Position-level detail:**
- USD/CNY (FX001, Revenue, JPMorgan): Gross USD 3,200M, hedged USD 1,400M (43.8%). This is the largest single FX exposure in the portfolio. The hedge ratio of 43.8% is the second-lowest across FX positions and is approaching the 45% breach threshold. Maturity 6 months. Unrealised P&L: USD -8.4M.
- USD/EUR (FX002, Revenue, Deutsche Bank): Gross USD 2,800M, hedged USD 1,200M (42.9%). This hedge ratio of 42.9% is the lowest across all FX positions and is BELOW the 45% breach threshold individually, though the aggregate portfolio ratio of 46.3% remains above breach. Maturity 12 months. Unrealised P&L: +USD 7.2M (the only FX position with a positive unrealised P&L).
- USD/JPY (FX003, Cost, HSBC): Gross USD 980M, hedged USD 600M (61.2%). This is the best-hedged FX position, above the 60% amber threshold. However, it carries the largest single FX unrealised loss at USD -15.6M. Maturity 3 months — shortest maturity in the FX book; rollover risk is near-term.
- USD/KRW (FX004, Cost, Citibank): Gross USD 620M, hedged USD 320M (51.6%). Hedge ratio is between amber and breach thresholds. Unrealised P&L: USD -9.8M. Maturity 6 months.

**Commodity positions (supplementary — not in F-01 KRI but relevant to EBITDA covenant stress):**
- DRAM (COM001, Goldman Sachs): Gross USD 890M, hedged USD 400M (44.9%), unrealised P&L USD -12.3M. Maturity 3 months.
- NAND (COM002, JPMorgan): Gross USD 650M, hedged USD 280M (43.1%), unrealised P&L USD -8.1M. Maturity 3 months.
- Combined commodity unrealised P&L: USD -20.4M, flowing through COGS → gross profit → EBITDA.

**Unrealised P&L split for covenant stress (traceable to hedge model):**
- FX positions only: USD -26.6M (flows through operating income → EBITDA)
- Commodity positions: USD -20.4M (flows through COGS → gross profit → EBITDA)
- Total portfolio: USD -47.0M

**Trend:** Deteriorating. Unhedged FX has crossed the amber threshold. The USD/EUR position hedge ratio of 42.9% is individually below the 45% breach level. USD/JPY and DRAM/NAND commodity positions mature in 3 months, creating near-term rollover risk. Control effectiveness data not provided in this run — confidence on trend is moderate.

---

### F-02: REVENUE CONCENTRATION & ACCOUNTS RECEIVABLE

**Top-customer concentration:** 22.1% (Enterprise Tier1 segment), above the 20% amber threshold and below the 25% breach threshold. Status: amber.

**Bad debt provision:** 1.44% of portfolio, against an amber threshold of 0.40% and breach threshold of 0.55%. Status: confirmed breach. This figure also triggers a COV006 covenant breach (threshold 0.80%) — see F-03 section.

**Segment-level AR aging detail:**
- Enterprise Tier1: Current USD 2,840M, overdue 30d USD 180M, overdue 60d USD 42M, overdue 90d USD 8M, provision USD 12.4M, concentration 22.1%.
- Enterprise Tier2: Current USD 1,620M, overdue 30d USD 210M, overdue 60d USD 68M, overdue 90d USD 18M, provision USD 28.6M, concentration 8.4%. The overdue 30d balance of USD 210M is the highest of any segment.
- SMB Tier3: Current USD 980M, overdue 30d USD 142M, overdue 60d USD 38M, overdue 90d USD 22M, provision USD 32.1M, concentration 2.1%. The highest absolute provision balance of any segment.
- Government Tier1: Current USD 640M, overdue 30d USD 28M, overdue 60d USD 4M, overdue 90d USD 0M, provision USD 2.8M, concentration 14.2%. Cleanest AR quality of all segments.
- Consumer Tier4: Current USD 420M, overdue 30d USD 68M, overdue 60d USD 24M, overdue 90d USD 12M, provision USD 18.4M, concentration 1.2%.

**Total 90d+ overdue:** USD 60.0M. Total bad debt provision across all segments: USD 94.3M (= USD 12.4M + 28.6M + 32.1M + 2.8M + 18.4M).

**Trend:** Deteriorating. Bad debt provision is more than 2.6× the breach threshold (1.44% vs 0.55%), which meets the 'severe breach' criterion. The provision is also 80% above the COV006 covenant threshold of 0.80%, with a cure cost (full write-off scenario) of USD ~94.3M that directly threatens COV001 EBITDA headroom.

---

### F-03: LIQUIDITY & REFINANCING

**Covenant-by-covenant status (2026-06-30 test date — most current):**

**COV001 — Net Debt/EBITDA (RCF-USD2B):** Current 2.8x vs threshold 3.0x. Ratio headroom: 0.2x. EBITDA headroom: USD 152M (derived: Net Debt = 2.8 × USD 2.28B = USD 6.384B; COV001 binding EBITDA floor = USD 6.384B / 3.0x = USD 2.128B; headroom = USD 2.28B − USD 2.128B = USD 0.152B = USD 152M). Status: amber. Notable deterioration: ratio moved from 2.6x (March test) to 2.8x (June test), consuming USD 100M+ of EBITDA headroom quarter-on-quarter. No active deleveraging programme referenced in provided data. Trend: deteriorating.

**COV002 — Interest Coverage (Term-Loan-A):** Current 4.2x vs threshold 3.5x. Headroom 0.7x. Status: ok. Declined from 4.8x (March) to 4.2x (June) — directional deterioration but still within comfortable range.

**COV003 — Liquidity Headroom (RCF-USD2B):** Current USD 1.1B vs threshold USD 0.8B. Headroom USD 0.3B. Status: ok. Declined from USD 1.2B (March) to USD 1.1B (June). Above the USD 0.9B escalation trigger. No escalation required on this covenant alone at current level, but trajectory is negative.

**COV004 — Debt Maturity Runway (Bond-2027):** Current 8 months vs threshold 12 months. Zero headroom. Status: amber. Runway improved from the March reading of 11 months (which was also below threshold) to 8 months — this is not an improvement; the runway is shortening as calendar time passes. Next test: 2026-12-01. The 8-month current runway does not yet breach the 6-month escalation trigger, but absent refinancing action, the trigger will be met within approximately 2 months of calendar time.

**COV005 — EBITDA (All-Facilities):** Current USD 2.28B vs threshold USD 1.80B. Headroom USD 0.48B. Status: ok. EBITDA declined from USD 2.51B (March) to USD 2.28B (June). Headroom compressed from USD 0.71B to USD 0.48B.

**COV006 — Bad Debt Provision % (Trade-Finance): CONFIRMED ACTIVE BREACH.** Current 1.44% vs threshold 0.80%. Negative headroom: -0.64 percentage points. This is not a forward probability — it is a confirmed breach requiring immediate cure or waiver action.

**COV006 cure implications:**
- Full write-off scenario: USD ~94.3M P&L charge. This would consume 62% of COV001 EBITDA headroom (USD 152M), leaving USD ~58M before COV001 Net Debt/EBITDA breach.
- Partial cure scenario (USD 50M write-off): EBITDA headroom falls to USD ~102M.
- Cross-default risk: if notification obligation is not fulfilled before the 2026-06-30 test date, cross-default provisions in the credit facility may be triggered.

**MANDATORY BOARD ACTION — COV006 LENDER NOTIFICATION:**
(a) BOARD DIRECTIVE REQUIRED: Lender notification is a board-authorised act under credit facility terms.
(b) Written board authorisation is required before the Group Treasurer contacts lenders regarding the COV006 breach.
(c) Hard deadline for board resolution: 2026-06-12.
(d) Named joint owners: Group Treasurer AND CFO (CFO co-signature required per facility terms).
(e) Cross-default risk: if notification obligation is not met before the 2026-06-30 test date, cross-default provisions across facilities may be triggered.

**Liquidity escalation check:** COV003 liquidity headroom = USD 1.1B, above the USD 0.9B escalation floor. Debt maturity runway = 8 months, above the 6-month escalation threshold. No individual escalation trigger for F-03 is formally breached on these two metrics at current values. However, COV006 is a confirmed covenant breach — escalation trigger 'Any covenant actually breached' is met.

---

### F-04: ACCOUNTING & FINANCIAL REPORTING

**AUD003 Internal-SOX (Financial Controls):** Completed. Open high findings: 1. Critical findings: 0. Material weaknesses: 0.

**Status assessment:** With 1 open high finding, the count is below the amber threshold of 2. Status: ok. No material weakness exists, so the material weakness escalation trigger is not met.

**Trend:** Flat. The audit cycle progressed from In-Progress to Completed status with a single high finding — below threshold, no escalation trigger met.

---

### CROSS-DOMAIN INTERCONNECTION ANALYSIS

1. **F-03 ↔ F-02 (COV006 cure destroying EBITDA headroom):** The COV006 breach is directly sourced from bad debt provision data (F-02). The cure cost (USD ~94.3M full write-off) flows through P&L and compresses COV001 EBITDA headroom from USD 152M to USD ~58M in the full scenario. This creates a cascading risk where resolving one breach (COV006) materially increases probability of a second breach (COV001).

2. **F-01 ↔ F-03 (FX/commodity mark-to-market → EBITDA):** Total portfolio unrealised P&L of USD -47.0M (FX: USD -26.6M, commodity: USD -20.4M), if realised, would further compress EBITDA. Combined with a COV006 full cure, total EBITDA stress could reach USD ~141.3M against USD 152M headroom — leaving only USD ~10.7M of buffer before COV001 breach. This scenario is not a forecast but illustrates the compound risk under concurrent realisation.

3. **F-02 ↔ F-03 (Enterprise Tier1 concentration):** Top customer concentration of 22.1% (Enterprise Tier1) means any deterioration in that single customer relationship would simultaneously worsen F-02 concentration KRI, increase bad debt provision (worsening COV006), and reduce revenue/EBITDA (worsening COV001 and COV005).

4. **F-01 ↔ F-03 (COV004 + USD/JPY rollover timing):** USD/JPY (FX003, HSBC) matures in 3 months, the same horizon as DRAM and NAND commodity hedges. COV004 Bond-2027 runway of 8 months means refinancing negotiations must commence imminently. Coincident hedge rollover needs and bond refinancing create compounded treasury execution risk, though no escalation threshold is currently breached on this basis alone.

---

### DATA GAPS & CONFIDENCE NOTES

- Control effectiveness percentages (control_effectiveness_pct) were not provided for F-01, F-02, or F-03 KRIs. Trend assessments for these risks are based on directional data signals (ratio movements, headroom compression, threshold proximity) rather than control effectiveness scores. Confidence on trend calls: moderate.
- No active deleveraging programme data was provided. COV001 trend is assessed as deteriorating based solely on ratio movement from 2.6x to 2.8x and compressed EBITDA headroom with no offsetting data.
- F-04 has only one audit cycle data point in the current run. Trend is assessed as flat based on the single completed audit finding count.

**Overall Status: BREACH (ai_audit_coverage_pct) | No Confirmed Violation Escalation Triggered**

**EU AI Act — ai_audit_coverage_pct:** Most recent value = 78.0% (source: compliance_metrics.csv). The stated breach threshold is <90%. At 78.0%, this KRI is in BREACH status. The EU AI Act becomes effective 2026-08-02 — approximately 66 days from the assessment date of 2026-05-28. The prior data row recorded 74.0%, meaning coverage has improved by 4 percentage points. However, at the current trajectory a 22-percentage-point gap to the 100% target (and 12-percentage-point gap to the breach threshold) with only ~66 days to the effective date represents a material compliance risk. The regulatory_horizon.csv entry confirms: gap = 'AI audit coverage at 78% vs 100% target', owner = CTO, status = In-Progress. **No confirmed AI Act violation has been reported (the Act's mandatory obligations take full effect 2026-08-02); however, the trajectory and proximity to the effective date makes this a priority remediation item.** Trend is classified as improving given the +4pp movement, but the KRI remains in breach.

**GDPR — gdpr_dsr_resolution_rate_pct:** Most recent value = 94.0% (source: compliance_metrics.csv). The input notes this as 'FYI context only, no threshold.' The regulatory_horizon.csv entry (most recent row) shows GDPR status = Compliant, with a gap note of 'DSR resolution rate 94% vs 95% target.' No formal KRI threshold breach is triggered. The 94% resolution rate means approximately 6% of Data Subject Requests under GDPR Article 12(3) are not resolved within the 30-day statutory deadline. This is a compliance quality concern even where no threshold breach is recorded. AUD004 (Privacy-GDPR Data-Processing) has been completed with 2 findings and 1 high-severity finding — the nature of these findings is not specified in the data provided. Confidence in the completeness of GDPR compliance is therefore assessed as medium: the audit findings are noted but not characterised, and a 94% DSR resolution rate represents 6% non-compliance with a statutory deadline under GDPR Article 12(3). No confirmed GDPR violation is recorded; no formal regulator investigation appears in audit_log.csv. No zero-tolerance escalation is triggered on current data.

**PIPL (China, effective 2021-11-01):** Status = Compliant (both data rows). Gap: Annual review due Q3 2026. No current compliance gap. No action required beyond scheduling the Q3 2026 review.

**No confirmed GDPR or PIPL violation is recorded. No regulator investigation appears in audit_log.csv. Zero-tolerance escalation thresholds for C-02 are not triggered.**

### C-03: ANTI-BRIBERY & ESG

**Overall Status: BREACH (third_party_abac_coverage) | No Confirmed Violation Escalation Triggered**

**Third-Party ABAC Coverage:** third_party_abac_coverage_pct = 89.0% (source: compliance_metrics.csv, C-03 data, most recent value). The stated breach threshold is <95%. At 89.0%, this KRI is in BREACH status. This gap is mapped to two distinct statutory obligations in regulatory_horizon.csv:
- **FCPA (US, effective 1977-12-19):** Status=Partial. Gap: 'Third-party coverage at 89% vs 100%.' Under the FCPA, inadequate third-party due diligence is a known enforcement risk factor, particularly for distributor relationships (Tier-2). Owner: CCO.
- **UK Bribery Act (UK, effective 2011-07-01):** Status=Partial. Gap: 'Third-party coverage at 89% vs 100%.' Section 7 of the UK Bribery Act creates a strict liability offence for failure of a commercial organisation to prevent bribery, with the 'adequate procedures' defence requiring demonstrable third-party controls. At 89% coverage, the adequate procedures defence is materially weakened. Owner: CCO.

AUD001 (Third-Party-ABAC, Tier-1 Suppliers) has been completed with 3 findings and 2 high-severity findings (upgraded from 0 findings in the prior data row). This is a significant escalation in audit findings within the Tier-1 supplier population and must be remediated under CCO ownership. AUD002 (Third-Party-ABAC, Tier-2 Distributors) is In-Progress with 0 findings to date, due 2026-06-30. The prior data row showed coverage at 85% and both FCPA/UK Bribery Act entries at 85%, consistent with improvement to 89% in the current period (+4pp). Trend is improving but KRI remains in breach.

**No confirmed FCPA or UK Bribery Act violation is recorded in the provided data.** Zero-tolerance escalation is not triggered on current data. However, the 2 high-severity findings in AUD001 for Tier-1 Suppliers require immediate CCO-led remediation and review to confirm they do not constitute or evidence FCPA/Bribery Act violations — this determination is not within the data provided and must be made by CCO/Legal.

**Whistleblower Cases:** whistleblower_high_findings = 1 (source: C-03 data input). AUD006 (Whistleblower-Cases All-Regions) shows findings=4, high=1, critical=0, ongoing, due 2026-12-31, owner=CCO. The prior data row showed findings=3, indicating one new finding has been added. The single high-severity whistleblower finding does not specify the seniority of any involved individual in the data provided. **If this finding involves an SVP-level or above individual, immediate escalation is required per zero-tolerance rules.** The data does not confirm or deny SVP-level involvement — this is a data gap. In a zero-tolerance domain, the absence of this data point is NOT grounds to assume no escalation is required. CCO must confirm the seniority level of any subject in the high-severity whistleblower case. Pending that confirmation, this finding is flagged as AMBER with a caveat that escalation status cannot be definitively resolved from current data. Confidence for this specific finding: low.

**CSRD — csrd_scope3_disclosure_pct:** Most recent value = 74.0% (source: compliance_metrics.csv). The input notes this as 'FYI context only — no KRI threshold.' The regulatory_horizon.csv entry records: CSRD status=In-Progress, effective=2024-01-01, gap='Scope 3 disclosure at 74% vs 80% target', owner=CSO. The 80% target is the internal programme benchmark; CSRD (EU Directive 2022/2464) is already effective. AUD007 (ESG-Disclosure Scope3-Emissions) is In-Progress with 0 findings, due 2026-09-30. Coverage improved from 70% to 74% (+4pp) indicating programme momentum. No KRI threshold breach is triggered; however, the gap to the 80% internal target remains open and the CSRD effective date has passed.

### DATA QUALITY AND CONFIDENCE NOTES

1. **Duplicate data rows:** Both C-01 screening_results.csv and C-02 compliance_metrics.csv contain two sets of rows (prior period and current period). All KRI values and status calls in this assessment use the most recent row unless specifically comparing trends.
2. **Whistleblower seniority data gap:** The high-severity finding in AUD006 does not specify the seniority level of any subject. CCO must confirm whether SVP-level or above individuals are involved. Confidence for the escalation status of this specific item: low.
3. **AUD004 high-severity finding nature:** The completed GDPR audit (AUD004) recorded 1 high-severity finding whose nature is not described in the data. CPO must confirm this finding does not constitute a confirmed GDPR violation. Until confirmed, this is a potential gap — not a confirmed violation per available data.
4. **AUD001 high-severity findings (x2):** Tier-1 ABAC audit completed with 2 high-severity findings. CCO/Legal must assess whether these constitute or evidence FCPA/UK Bribery Act violations. Not a confirmed violation per available data, but requires immediate legal review.
5. **DORA/NIS2 ownership boundary:** Both regulations are mapped to C-01 in regulatory_horizon.csv but the gap owner is CISO and the remediation (IT incident response testing) is squarely within the Operational Agent's domain (O-02). This agent flags the compliance obligation; it does not assess IT security implications. Chief Risk Agent should confirm cross-domain ownership routing to ensure neither agent treats this as solely the other's responsibility.

board_summary:
RISK POSTURE

The organisation records 17 KRI breaches and 18 amber warnings across all four risk domains. The systemic risk flag is active: Strategic, Operational, Financial, and Compliance domains are simultaneously in breach, indicating enterprise-wide compound exposure.

STRATEGIC: One breach and two amber warnings are recorded, with the Chief Executive Officer accountable. KRI S-01 has breached threshold on five detected geopolitical signals, triggering formal escalation. Compound scenario S-01+F-01 links geopolitical signals to elevated FX exposure requiring review against current hedging positions. Compound scenario S-03+F-02 flags three active deals — CloudScale Systems, NeuralEdge AI, and DataVault Security — as potentially shifting customer revenue concentration.

OPERATIONAL: Ten breaches and seven amber warnings are recorded, the heaviest breach count across all domains. Accountability is distributed across the COO, CISO, and CHRO by sub-domain. Add the following text after the sentence on Quanta's metrics: 'Supply continuity risk is modelled using Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) calculated as MTBF = max(15 × (health_score/100)², 2 years), where health_score is the financial health metric (0–100). This functional form and 15-year scalar have not yet been validated against supplier historical disruption frequency data or published industry MTBF benchmarks; the COO and VP Supply Chain are required to obtain such validation data within 30 days and report calibration results to the Board.' Cyber resilience assessment covers 0% of the supply chain, while 62.6% of USD 12,610M spend flows through single-source or concentrated suppliers, leaving a jointly undefended supplier perimeter with Add disclosure: 'The USD 2,634M VaR-95 interruption estimate is based on a Supply Chain model using demand_shock_probability of 24%/yr and severity of 15%; calibration basis for these parameters and their source data (historical demand-shock frequency, macro scenario assumptions) are to be documented by COO/Model Risk within 15 business days per MO-05 remediation.' (1-year horizon; CVaR-95 USD 3,236M). IT-Operations annualised attrition of 11.8% and seven critical roles open beyond 60 days directly impair remediation of eight critical and 23 high vulnerabilities open beyond 30 days, a patch compliance rate of only 71%, and an MTTD breach at 11 days. Three R&D flight-risk employees departing would erode product safety knowledge, compounding a recall readiness score of 72 with the last simulation conducted eight months ago. The AUD004 Privacy-GDPR audit completed with two findings including one high-severity; to the extent any finding involves IT systems processing personal data, cross-domain notification to the O-02 Operational agent is required. DORA, effective 17 January 2025, and NIS2, effective 18 October 2024, both carry status Partial in the regulatory horizon, with the documented gap of an untested IT incident response plan unresolved past their effective dates. Ownership is formally assigned: CISO as primary operational remediation owner (O-02), CCO as compliance assurance owner and governance steward (C-01). This dual-ownership model is to be recorded in the governance log and confirmed in the next executive recommendation cycle by 2026-06-14 to eliminate cross-domain ambiguity.

FINANCIAL: Four breaches and seven amber warnings are recorded, with the Chief Financial Officer accountable. The bad debt provision breach on COV006 stands at 1.44% against a 0.80% threshold, driven by Tier-2 Enterprise at USD 28.6M, SMB Tier-3 at USD 32.1M, and Consumer Tier-4 at USD 18.4M; a full cure write-off of approximately USD 94.3M would consume 62% of COV001 EBITDA headroom of USD 152M, compressing Net Debt/EBITDA toward the 3.0x breach threshoThe combined FX and commodity unrealised loss totals USD 47.0M on a gross notional basis, comprising FX positions of USD -26.6M across USD/CNY, USD/EUR, USD/JPY, and USD/KRW (reported on gross notional USD 7,600M; hedged notional USD 3,520M yields USD -12.0M), and commodity positions of USD -20.4M across DRAM and NAND. The FX figures overstate hedged portfolio loss by approximately USD 14.6M; if the gross notional unrealised losses are realised as currently reported, EBITDA headroom could fall to as little as USD 105M before COV001 breach, but if hedged-notional FX losses are used, the worst-case headroom compresses to USD 25.3M, materially above the USD 10.7M figure. Group Treasurer must restate FX003 and FX004 P&L using hedged notional immediately and relock the worst-case headroom bridge before the next board run.case, after COV006 cure of USD 94.3M and realisation of USD 47.0M unrealised loss) before COV001 breach. USD/CNY unhedged exposure of USD 1,800M — gross USD 3,200M less hedged USD 1,400M — is the single largest unhedged position; CNY depreciation would increase unrealised losses beyond the current USD -8.4M, with COV004 Bond-2027 maturity runway of only eight months creating concurrent refinancing pressure while EBITDA is under stress. Enterprise Tier-1 concentration at 22.1% of revenue from a single customer is simultaneously pressuring the Trade-Finance facility and the COV001 EBITDA covenant alongside the COV006 bad debt breach.

COMPLIANCE: Two breaches and two amber warnings are recorded, with the Chief Compliance Officer and General Counsel jointly accountable. Two export licences — ECC-2024-0047 covering TSMC components and ECC-2024-0061 covering DRAM modules — expire within 30 days and share the same owner as the ABAC remediation programme. Concurrent CCO workload across AUD002 Tier-2 ABAC due 30 June 2026, AUD005 Export-Control audit due 30 June 2026, and the two licence renewals creates capacity risk that could delay resolution of both C-01 and C-03 obligations simultaneously.

KEY RISK DRIVERS

The most consequential combination of findings this period is the intersection of a confirmed covenant breach, a deteriorating cyber posture, and a structurally fragile supply chain — three conditions that are individually material and collectively compounding. The COV006 covenant breach is confirmed today: the bad debt provision of 1.44% exceeds both the internal management action threshold of 0.55% and the COV006 lender covenant threshold of 0.80%, placing the company in active breach of its Trade Finance facility with a hard board deadline of 12 June 2026 for lender notification. The financial picture is more acute than gross headroom figures suggest. COV001 gross headroom is USD 152.0M; after applying the full COV006 cure provision of USD 94.3M, effective headroom falls to USD 57.7M; and if the total portfolio unrealised loss of USD 47.0M — comprising USD 26.6M on FX positions and USD 20.4M on commodity positions — is realised, combined worst-case COV001 headroom compresses to USD 10.7M ahead of the 30 June 2026 test date. Separately, the probability of an additional COV001 Net Debt/EBITDA breach at that test date is more likely than not at 50.6% — provisional pending a full model rerun due 20 June 2026 — and this figure is conditional on COV006 remaining in breach. Against this financial backdrop, the cyber domain presents six simultaneous control failures: mean time to detect has risen to 11 days against a 10-day threshold, patch compliance has fallen to 71% against an 80% threshold, IT recovery time is at its 6-hour limit, MFA coverage is below threshold at 87%, privileged access has gone unreviewed for 45 days, and supplier cyber resilience assessments stand at 0%. That final figure — zero assessments completed across a supply base where 62.6% of USD 12,610M in spend flows through single-source or concentrated suppliers — is the point where the cyber failure becomes a supply chain failure. The commodity hedge ratio at 44.2% is in breach of its 45% threshold, adding a further financial pressure point, while the FX hedge ratio at 46.3% remains amber, well below the 60% policy target. The Chief Financial Officer must present the lender notification and COV006 cure strategy to the Board by 12 June 2026.


CROSS-DOMAIN CONNECTIONS

The most direct cross-domain amplification runs from talent through cyber to supply chain. Seven critical roles have been open for more than 60 days in breach of the six-role threshold, and IT-Operations attrition is running at 11.8%. This staffing erosion is not an abstract HR concern: it is directly impairing the organisation's ability to close the 29-percentage-point patch compliance gap, remediate eight critical vulnerabilities open beyond 30 days, and reduce mean time to detect from its current breach level. If the organisation cannot staff its cyber remediation programme, the six concurrent O-02 control failures will persist — and with zero supplier cyber assessments completed, a compromise propagating through an unassessed supplier such as Foxconn or TSMC, both single-source and together representing a substantial portion of the concentrated spend base, would trigger a supply interruption valued under stress at USD 2,634M in a bad year. The cyber and talent agents flagged this connection individually; the board should understand it as a single chain: attrition degrades cyber controls, cyber controls fail to protect an unassessed supplier perimeter, and the supply chain consequence is potentially catastrophic. A second amplification runs through the financial domain. The five geopolitical signals now in breach — three of which are high severity, including expansion of the US Entity List and increased Taiwan Strait military exercises — directly elevate the probability of a Taiwan corridor disruption. The organisation holds only 3.9 weeks of inventory cover against an 18-week TSMC lead time, meaning any Taiwan corridor disruption cannot be absorbed before a production gap of multiple quarters materialises. That same geopolitical deterioration puts upward pressure on the USD 1,800M unhedged USD/CNY revenue exposure, any further losses on which flow directly into EBITDA and further compress the USD 57.7M post-cure COV001 headroom. The S-01 geopolitical breach, the O-01 supply concentration breach, and the F-03 covenant stress are therefore mutually reinforcing. A third connection concerns the cyber and talent interaction in revenue-critical functions: with tech attrition at 14.2% and seven critical roles unfilled, the possibility that flight-risk staff includes personnel on the trading or treasury desk cannot be dismissed. A concurrent active cyber incident — mean time to detect currently at 11 days, mean time to recover at 18 days — combined with the loss of revenue-critical staff during that window would create a direct revenue loss scenario, not merely an IT control failure. The compliance domain adds a further capacity constraint: the Chief Compliance Officer is concurrently accountable for two export licence renewals within 30 days, the Tier-2 ABAC audit due 30 June 2026, the export control audit due the same date, and the AI audit coverage gap with the EU AI Act Article 50 transparency obligations effective 2 August 2026. This workload concentration in a single owner creates a realistic risk that one or more of these obligations slips, with FCPA, UK Bribery Act, and EU AI Act exposure as the consequence.


QUARTER-ON-QUARTER MOVEMENT

Aggregate residual risk has risen materially from FY26Q1 to FY26Q2. Of 20 KRIs tracked across the comparison period, 15 are deteriorating in trend direction, three are improving, and two are stable — a ratio that confirms broad-based worsening rather than isolated pockets. The dominant cause is inherent risk growth: the external threat environment has expanded faster than the organisation's control posture has changed. The most visible examples are the geopolitical signal count moving from 2 to 5 (amber to breach) as the US Entity List expanded and Taiwan Strait military activity intensified, inventory cover falling from 4.3 to 3.9 weeks (amber to breach) as buffer stock was consumed, and the bad debt provision rising from 1.24% to 1.44% (remaining in breach but worsening) as customer credit quality deteriorated across three segments. Four KRIs entered breach status this period that were not in breach last period: mean time to detect (9 to 11 days), critical open roles beyond 60 days (5 to 7), inventory cover (4.3 to 3.9 weeks), and geopolitical signal count (2 to 5). Control weakening is also present — patch compliance has continued to fall within breach (78% to 71%), and supplier cyber resilience assessments have deteriorated further within breach (15% to 0%) — but inherent risk growth is the dominant driver this period. The single KRI whose reversal would most reduce residual risk is mean time to detect: restoring MTTD to within threshold would directly reduce the dwell time available to a threat actor in an environment where patch compliance is low, privileged access is unreviewed, and the supplier perimeter is entirely unassessed. This is the board's primary decision point.

RISK COMMITTEE RECOMMENDED ACTIONS:
  • Direct the CFO to notify the lending syndicate of the COV006 covenant breach immediately and present a fully costed cure strategy to the Board no later than 12 June 2026 (action_deadline per cross_default_risk KRI); the committee must be informed that COV006 is confirmed in active breach today (certainty), that effective COV001 headroom post-cure is USD 57.7M (not USD 152.0M gross), that combined worst-case headroom after portfolio MTM losses is USD 10.7M, and that the probability of an additional COV001 breach at the 30 June 2026 test is 50.6% PROVISIONAL (more likely than not; full model rerun due 2026-06-20) — inaction on COV006 cure risks cross-default and loss of access to the lending facility.
  • Direct the CISO to present an emergency remediation plan at the next meeting addressing all six concurrent cyber control failures: MTTD 11 days (breach), patch compliance 71% (breach), IT RTO 6 hours (breach, at limit), MFA coverage 87% (breach), privileged access unreviewed 45 days (breach, at limit), and supplier cyber resilience assessments at 0% (breach); the plan must also address the O-01+O-02 compound scenario — with 62.6% of supply spend in single-source or concentrated suppliers and zero supplier assessments completed, the worst-case supply interruption exposure is USD 2,634M — and must include the CISO's response to the O-02+O-04 staffing constraint with 7 critical roles open more than 60 days impairing remediation capacity.
  • Require the CFO / Group Treasurer to present a hedging remediation plan addressing the commodity hedge ratio breach at 44.2% (below the 45% threshold) and the deteriorating FX hedge ratio at 46.3% (below the 60% policy target); the plan must quantify the path to restoring both ratios within policy, reference the combined worst-case stress exposure of USD 924M to USD 1,258M, and account for the direct link between portfolio unrealised losses (currently USD 47.0M combined) and COV001 headroom, which in combined worst-case stands at USD 10.7M.
  • Direct the Chief Operating Officer / VP Supply Chain to deliver, within 30 days, a concrete supplier diversification and inventory buffer plan that addresses: single-source concentration at 62.6% (breach), inventory cover at 3.9 weeks (breach, deteriorating), geographic concentration at 75.3% (breach), and the immediate commencement of supplier cyber resilience assessments (currently 0%, breach); the plan must specifically address the Quanta [SUP005] compound risk — financial health score 61, rejection rate 1.2%, minimum inventory cover — given simultaneous O-01 and O-03 exposure on the ODM-Laptops line.
  • Require the Chief Compliance Officer / General Counsel to present a workload triage plan addressing the concurrent compliance obligations: AI audit coverage at 78% (breach, threshold 90%); third-party ABAC coverage at 89% (breach, threshold 95%); two export licences expiring within 30 days; and the DORA and NIS2 obligations already past their effective dates with the IT incident response plan gap unresolved — the committee must confirm ownership routing between the CCO and CISO for DORA/NIS2 remediation to ensure no obligation falls between domains, and must note that concurrent CCO workload across AUD002, AUD005, and licence renewals creates a capacity risk that could delay resolution of both C-01 and C-03 simultaneously.

cov001_board_narrative.text: IMPORTANT — COV001 AMBER RATIO VS FORWARD BREACH PROBABILITY: The Net Debt to EBITDA ratio currently reads 2.8× (amber — below the 3.0× breach ceiling). This ratio reflects the position as at the Q2 reporting date and is correct. However, the EBITDA Stress Monte Carlo separately estimates a 50.5% probability that this ratio will exceed 3.0× (model_params authoritative MC value using effective headroom USD 57.7M — 2026-06-09; original figure 69.4% was a calculation error corrected 2026-06-09) by the 2026-06-30 test date — a breach-status forward indicator. These two signals are not contradictory: the ratio measures where the organisation is today; the Monte Carlo measures where it is likely to be in 21 days under plausible EBITDA scenarios. Both signals must be read together. The amber ratio does NOT indicate the covenant position is under control — the forward probability of 50.5% means more likely than not that a COV001 breach will occur at the next test date. The board should treat this as a breach-trajectory situation requiring the same urgency as an active breach. A deterministic scenario in which the COV006 provision charge of USD 94.3M (corrected; prior USD 365M was a calculation error) recognised in full would reduce EBITDA to approximately USD 2,185.7M — USD 57.7M above the COV001 implied floor of USD 2,128M — meaning the cure alone does not deterministically breach COV001, but the Monte Carlo shows P(breach) = 50.5% under plausible EBITDA scenarios at test date.
